questions.stem: A team runs a serverless Azure Cosmos DB account and now needs it to serve low-latency reads from three continents while surviving a regional outage. What must change first before any additional regions can be associated?

options.text: Enable availability zones on the existing serverless account
Convert the account to provisioned (or autoscale) throughput
Redeploy the application with a multi-region connection string
Add the new regions during a scheduled maintenance window

questions.explanation: Serverless accounts are single-region only, so global distribution is impossible until the account uses provisioned throughput; only then can additional regions be associated. Adding regions itself needs no redeploy or downtime.

questions.stem: A single-write-region account has service-managed failover enabled but no failover priority order configured. The write region goes down. What happens?

options.text: The lowest-priority read region is promoted to write automatically
All read regions are promoted to write simultaneously
No region is promoted automatically, so a manual forced failover is required
The original write region auto-resumes as soon as it recovers

questions.explanation: Automatic promotion needs both the feature enabled and a ranked failover priority list; with no ranked target, the reliable fix during the outage is a manual forced failover to a healthy region.

questions.stem: During a normal maintenance window with all regions healthy, an operations team wants to move the write region from East US to West Europe with no data loss. Which operation fits?

options.text: Forced (customer-managed) failover of the write region
Service-managed failover
Change write region (planned failover)
Disable service-managed failover and delete the East US region

questions.explanation: With all regions healthy, change write region lets replication catch up before promoting West Europe, so there is no data loss; a forced failover would risk losing unreplicated writes and planned failover cannot run during an actual outage.

questions.stem: Which sequence lists the five Azure Cosmos DB consistency levels correctly from strongest to weakest?

options.text: Strong, Session, Bounded staleness, Consistent prefix, Eventual
Strong, Bounded staleness, Session, Consistent prefix, Eventual
Bounded staleness, Strong, Session, Eventual, Consistent prefix
Strong, Bounded staleness, Consistent prefix, Session, Eventual

questions.explanation: The defined order from strongest to weakest is Strong, Bounded staleness, Session, Consistent prefix, Eventual, with Session as the account default.

questions.stem: A social app must ensure each user always sees their own most recent post immediately, while other users can tolerate slight delays. Which consistency level is the best fit?

options.text: Eventual
Consistent prefix
Bounded staleness
Session

questions.explanation: Session consistency guarantees read-your-writes within the user's own session via the session token, so the author always sees their new post while keeping low latency and high throughput for everyone else; it is also the account default.

questions.stem: Which statements about the impact of consistency level on request unit cost and availability are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Strong and Bounded staleness reads cost roughly 2x the RUs of the weaker levels
Write RU cost is identical across all consistency levels for a given operation
Strong consistency is supported together with multi-region writes
Weaker levels such as Session, Consistent prefix, and Eventual offer higher availability and throughput
Strong reads consume the same RUs as Eventual reads

questions.explanation: Strong and Bounded staleness reads use a two-replica local quorum (~2x RUs), write RU cost does not vary by level, and weaker levels give higher availability and throughput. Strong is not supported with multi-region writes, and Strong reads cost more than Eventual reads.

questions.stem: In which scenario does Azure Cosmos DB fail to meet the usual sub-10 ms P99 latency guarantee?

options.text: Session reads served from a single region
Eventual writes replicated across two regions
Strong writes that must commit across multiple regions
Bounded staleness reads served from a single region

questions.explanation: Only Strong multi-region writes break the sub-10 ms P99 guarantee, because the write must commit in every region first; its latency is roughly 2x RTT + 10 ms between the farthest regions. Reads stay under 10 ms P99 at every level.

questions.stem: Which statements accurately describe multi-region writes (active-active) in Azure Cosmos DB? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Every associated region can accept writes (active-active)
The topology provides 99.999% read and write availability
Strong consistency can be enabled to guarantee correctness
Concurrent writes to the same item in different regions can conflict
Bounded staleness is the recommended consistency level

questions.explanation: Multi-region writes make every region writable, deliver 99.999% read+write availability, and allow cross-region conflicts that require a conflict-resolution policy. Strong consistency is not supported and Bounded staleness is an anti-pattern with multi-region writes.

questions.stem: A new API for NoSQL container must resolve concurrent cross-region updates by keeping the record with the highest application-supplied version number, using no server-side code. Which configuration fits?

options.text: Custom policy with a registered merge stored procedure
Last Write Wins using the default _ts timestamp
Last Write Wins with a custom numeric conflict-resolution path such as /version
Change the existing container's policy to LWW after creation

questions.explanation: In Last Write Wins you can name a custom numeric conflict-resolution path where the highest value wins, with no stored procedure. It must be set at container creation because the policy is immutable afterward.

questions.stem: Which single-account configuration best meets Tailwind Global's requirements?

options.text: Single write region with Strong consistency and service-managed failover
Multi-region writes with Session consistency and a Last Write Wins conflict-resolution policy
Multi-region writes with Strong consistency and a custom merge stored procedure
Single write region with Bounded staleness and an ordered preferred-region list

questions.explanation: Only multi-region writes give lowest local read and write latency in every region plus survival of a regional outage; Session is a supported level for active-active, and Last Write Wins resolves the acceptable cross-region conflicts deterministically. Strong consistency is incompatible with multi-region writes.
